Everyone is deluged with a steady stream of calls and emails. How can you reach out to people and get a chance to present your solution to their key problems? Well, when done properly, cold email can be an effective way to identify the correct contact and put that person into a position where he or she must take your call or meeting. Who are the people and companies that can make a huge impact on your business? Well, today we're going to talk about how you can reach them and get the opportunity to present your presentation with a simple email message. I'm Robert Scrove, president of the Information Marketing Association and my guest today is Brian Kruzberger, the founder of Breakthrough Email. He helps companies and sellers grow their revenue. Breakthrough Email is the number one recommended cold emailing system. He's been featured in the New York Times and Forbes says he's one of the top 40 sales experts. In the last 18 months, his clients have generated over $32 million in new business and some of the clients include McDonald's, GE, Best Buy, Home Depot and MasterCard. Hello, come Brian. So glad you're on the program today. Hey, thanks so much for inviting me. Well, we're going to cover a lot of things today. It's a pretty ambitious agenda. We're going to, you know, how to turn an email into a sales conversation. How to use existing leverage within an organization to your favor. How to put the appropriate contact at your prospective company to position where he or she has to take your call. The common mistakes with cold email and some key applications to help you track your email messages after you click that send button. But Brian, let's start out. What is, you know, is it really possible in this world to spam and, and, and, and the fact, I mean, I'm getting all kinds of email and I'm deleting, you know, if I've got 25 emails in my box, I'm probably deleting 20 or 23 of them without even reading them each morning. Is email really an effective way to get through to decision makers? Absolutely. I mean, I think how emails typically done, it's not effective, right? Because people don't really spend much time on what they're going to write. They don't create an opportunity for the other person, right? They write about themselves and hope that the other person will respond or they'll, you know, kind of carpet bomb and send out thousands of emails to, you know, more and more people and be really frustrated that they're getting response rates of like one to, you know, 5% whatever it may be. But the key is to actually have a lot of thought and a process into the email. And if you, you know, as you said, you had 25 emails. If you were one of the, you know, top two or three emails that create an opportunity for the other person, then, you know, then you'll get responses. And there's a bunch of tips and tricks, you know, that we've kind of developed over the years. So how did you get started? What was kind of led you to using this as a marketing tool? Yes, I was in sales and selling for, you know, some startup technology companies. And in my 20s, living in New York City. And, you know, my problem always was how do I get a meeting? You know, how do I get a lead? And I had, you know, kind of a complicated software product for advertising. And I was literally going against a thousand of the sellers trying to reach out to, you know, different like CMOs and VP's marketing agencies selling advertising. So like McDonald's and backpices, GE's and, you know, these guys are deluged with like, literally, I mean, I talked to some of them later, you know, like a hundred phone calls a week and, you know, five or six hundred, you know, like a hundred emails a day. And they were just totally fed up with all the sellers and the outreach. And, you know, it was one day, it was probably like 2008. And I had written an email to Best Buy and I met my buddies in New York City at a restaurant and they had gone to the bathroom and, you know, you're kind of always checking your phone as a blackberry at the time. And I get this email back from Best Buy and it was unlike anything I'd ever seen. It was like, you know, I said the email, I think like the day before, that Brian, thank you so much for your email. You know, I'm the correct person to talk to. I, you know, I'd love to learn more about your services. You know, we have a weekly team call, you know, marketing call. I'd love it if you could come in and present, you know, all be your point of contact. You know, our weekly team calls Thursday at two o'clock, you know, Easter. You know, here are a few, you know, upcoming times that, you know, we'd be available. Additionally, if you're ever in Minneapolis, we'd love you to come in and meet. And like my normal email is like, please cease and desist. You go from that to come on down when you're in town. Yeah, exactly. And is it okay if I swear or is it like a no swearing policy? Probably fewer swear, fewer is better. Okay. So it's just like, holy, you know, expletive. This is amazing. Like, you know, what did I do differently in this email versus everything else? And, you know, and fast forward to what happened. I knew that, you know, this is like the VP of marketing at Best Buy. And there was a list of people to see them now, which is like six more people. And, you know, I looked them all up and it's all the, you know, the head marketing people across the country for Best Buy. And, you know, I obviously told them, like, listen, hey, I'll be in Minneapolis. It's like in 10 days, we'd love to come meet you. How does this look? So I showed up in Minneapolis, met with several of them and had like a $45,000 deal within about 50 days, about a month and a half. And my normal sales cycle is like, you know, nine months to, you know, a year and a half kind of thing. And in that kind of deal size, it was actually kind of small. But the speed had never happened before like that. That's brilliant. Now, I mean, this is something for information marketers. You go, well, I'm not trying to reach Best Buy, but you could very well be trying to reach a joint venture partner, a potential sponsor for your events, a speaker that you want to attract that has a, that is really going to draw a lot of people to your event. And there are times when you're trying to do this outreach, maybe it's maybe somebody who has a platform that you would like to speak at, or, you know, there's all these times that you're trying to do outreach to busy people that have lots of gatekeepers, so to speak. Sometimes, you know, folks who are reading their email and deleting messages on their behalf. And if you can, you know, use these techniques in order to get a response, it can completely change your business. Yeah, I mean, the idea is anybody who you want to connect with, you know, probably in the next two hours, we'll check their email. You know, like any CEO, any, you know, VP of whatever, any celebrity, any, like, whomever, I mean, everyone's connected their email. And the majority of people have not outsourced their email because it's not like, you know, they don't give it to their assistants to handle all their email because it's personal, it's business, it's internal, it's clients, it's everybody. And, yeah, I mean, you can literally connect with almost anybody in the world over email, even like President Obama didn't want to give up his Black card, you know, he became president. Yeah, see, and he didn't. The Secret Service found a way to secure it and he's got it, or he's got whatever device he has now. So the key is, you know, how do you, how do you write something effective, you know, how do you write an effective email that gets them to respond and, you know, it actually gets the action that you want. And you have to put, I literally sent 10,000, I sent 10,000 individual emails to develop the system. Wow. And, you know, we just, I constantly was testing, you know, year after year after year, like what work would people respond to, what didn't they respond to? And, you know, we had, you know, we've had pretty much anybody that I wanted to meet with or reach out to, you know, respond to it. And we'll get, you know, for the people that go through our cold emailing program, you know, the break for email, the, you know, the average response rate is 80%. Oh my goodness. Now that's pretty amazing because, you know, most folks, it's send an email and then you don't hear anything. And that's pretty miraculous that simply changing the way you write the email can make all the difference in the response. And I guess that's what copywriting is all about. So what are some of the elements of emails that get a response? How do you draft an email that makes people want to reply? And reach out kind of the way Best Buy did to you, invite you in? So, well, I'll take a quick step back and then we'll get to that question. Like if you look at the alternatives, right, there's, you know, this is like outbound, right? There's inbound marketing, you know, which is SEO and, you know, content and, you know, everything and content marketing, anything else you can do. And then outbound and within outbound, primarily people cold call, right? They call you up, you don't know the number and you, you know, they leave a voicemail or they keep calling you and they keep bugging you. And the problem with, you know, with outbound, especially cold calling is, you know, you don't have permission to talk to them, right? They haven't, like I said, you know, please give me a call. You don't have like a scheduled time to talk and you're interrupting their day, right? So somebody called me right now or you right now, we're in the middle of something totally different and, you know, and from there, they'll, at least for me, at least like when I was, you know, cold calling people, I would, you know, stumble over my words. And, you know, I just, I wasn't comfortable. I never felt like I had this canned elevator pitch that was going to, you know, persuade or convince anybody to, you know, want to meet with me or want to talk to me. And, you know, I did it for over a year and I still, you know, I can call, call, I know fear. But I just found that if I made 100 calls, probably only four or five would pick up the phone out of 100 and I, you know, and I would track this. And, you know, within, say, the 95 voicemails, very few, if any, would ever, you know, call me back. I was like shocked that somebody actually called me back. So, you know, what I, and I couldn't delegate this to somebody else because, you know, they had to be, you know, pretty, pretty skilled, you know, to actually communicate what it is we do. Now, the thing with email that everybody does is they, you know, they write about themselves. They send out lots of emails. They copy and paste, you know, a bunch of bad emails like, you know, hi, my name is Brian Kruzberger. I work with Breakthrough Email. I'm the founder of BreakthroughEmail.com, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. Let me tell you about my company. And, you know, within that, I made three major mistakes. But, you know, so the key, the real key is David Pink wrote a book, where Daniel Pink, David Pink, I think, I can't think of it, but like, Selling Is Human. And he talked about attunement. And, you know, the key lesson that I learned was, let me close my eyes and like, think about this other person, like, what's going on in their day. Right. And the people I was reaching out to at the time were saying like, VP's in marketing or CMO's or VP's of advertising at, you know, Fortune 5500 type companies. And, you know, they're busy in the day. Like, they get there. They have all these emails, you know, coming to them. They have all these phone calls, all this other stuff that they have to do. They have a family. They have kids. You know, the last thing they really want to do is go to work. You know, they're kind of waiting until, you know, the weekend or the kids soccer game. And everyone is saying, you know, I want something from you. Right. Right. If you like, think of those 25 emails, how many of those emails were even like, personally written only for you, do you think? None. Well, some of them were, I'd imagine, as you responded to them. Well, I guess on a given day, well, the ones I, oh, the ones that, well, probably the two or three that I responded to. The other ones were not. They were just general kind of blasts out to everybody. And they even said things like, I hope you all, you know, it was obvious that they were, even by their language, that they were sending it to a whole bunch of people. Right. And so it's very easy for people to cut the email before you even, you know, before it even gets there. Right. So, you know, if you think about the email, you have a subject line, and now you have the preview lines, which a lot of people like don't even think about or talk about. So like, you know, they get to the point quick between that subject and the first two sentences. And, you know, the last thing I want to do is waste my time on saying, my name is Brian Kruzberger. I am the founder of BreakthroughEmail.com, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. Because that's in the, you know, in the subject, you know, it'll say Brian Kruzberger and whatever my subject is. And, you know, in the context of email, like they know in the signature that, you know, that the name of the person and their company is all going to be there. So, you know, why waste my time on something that they don't care about? Excellent. Now, so one of the keys is not talking about you, not, you know, not being just one more of, you know, one more person that's kind of asking for something from them. How do you turn that around? So, all right. So I'm just going to go through like the watercol technique and one of the email templates we use. So if you think about, say, an organization, either a JV, you know, group, like I've done, I'm doing partnerships with like HubSpot and Salesforce and, you know, send emails to Tim Ferriss and, you know, you call me back, the author of Four Hour Workweek, anyone familiar with that? And doing a lot of joint ventures with people, partnerships. And, you know, the first thing you have to find out is, you know, who's the right person for you to talk to in the organization? And, you know, and I found that, and now, you know, I coach all these different, you know, salespeople, entrepreneurs, salespeople, that the number one mistake people make is they get a meeting or they write to the wrong people in the organization or they assume that the person that they're, you know, writing to is the right point of contact. Like in my company, and we have a, you know, small company, five people, I don't handle the, the partnership stuff. Like I have one of the ladies that I work with, Laura, she actually, you know, Laura at BreakthroughEmail.com, she, if anybody wants to reach out to her, but she's the one that handles all the JVs. Right. So somebody writes to me, I forward it over to Laura. Excellent. And that's in a very, very small, you know, company, very small organization. And if you think about even larger organization, like the roles and responsibilities, like that takes a long time to figure out who's who. And that's really the first thing that you're up against, like, how do you find the right point of contact? So in the case of Best Buy, I used something I now, I didn't know it was going to be time, but I call it the waterfall technique. And essentially, I would write to a couple of people above my point of contact. So at the time, I was meeting with, you know, advertising agencies who do all media buying, and I found that at the end of the sales cycle, they'd say, you know, hey, we got to present this to the client. We'll let you know. And I was losing deals because, you know, I found out they weren't presenting the opportunity to the client. And, you know, so I realized, like, okay, if I keep working with the ad agencies, like everybody else does, I'm going to lose these, you know, these deals. And essentially, it's going to be nine months of my time and a lot of heartache. So why don't go direct to the client? And, but the client's not going to take the meeting. Right. So like, say my target's the director of advertising at Best Buy, who like, in that who was the end decision maker. So I would write to the director of advertising, but I'd also write to the VP of advertising, the VP of marketing and the CMO. And I would tell them all that I wrote to all the other people. And what would happen is the VP of marketing or the, you know, the CMO would refer me down to the appropriate person. By the way, that's, that's the subject line of this email appropriate person. And they delegate it to the other person because I know if you think about the organization that you're writing to, what are the internal, like, characteristics at the company? Right. And nothing to do with me, but it had a lot to do with me thinking about them and what's going on. And everyone of the people, like, once they do a good job for the other people, like before their boss or their manager, and, you know, half of their job is, you know, kind of covering their butt to make sure they look good for, you know, the person that they report into. So if that person says, Hey, I want you to meet with Brian, you know, guess who's got a meeting? And so that's, that's the waterfall technique. And, you know, there's, that's one of the, one of the nine steps. And I think that's pretty neat because that puts pressure on, you know, so now it isn't Brian asking for the meeting, but it's the guy's boss saying, Hey, this, you know, why don't you check out and see what Brian has. And now the guy's got to take the meeting because he knows that at a, at a upcoming meeting, he's going to have to explain what the meet, what, what his conversation with Brian was like. He probably doesn't have to explain it. Right. He doesn't have to, you know, at this point, it's not like, Hey, you know, meet with Brian and tell me what it is. And, you know, I want to fold each other report. Right. The email typically say something like, looks interesting, talk to Bill Smith, or Bill Smith is the right guy. Right. And, you know, and from there I'd write Bill and say, you know, Hey, you know, Richie Hecker thought it would make sense for us to talk, you know, how does your calendar look? And, you know, and I've tested those two frasers and, you know, I know from there I'll probably get a 75% 75% response rate for that with the referral and, you know, setting up the calendar. But what's really at risk here for the guy who's referred is if he's not going to meet with me, he has to come up with a reason why not. And because people in organizations, you know, when you're playing, you know, social politics or, you know, corporate politics, you know, they're very risk adverse. So I use everything that was kind of a disadvantage for me, which is a really complicated org chart. It takes a long time to figure out who, you know, the decision maker is losing sales because I mean, with the wrong decision maker who, you know, won't let me meet with the true decision maker and losing any kind of leverage and then turn that into my favor. And that's why, you know, with a lot of sales people that I consult, I'm not sure, you know, with a lot of the sales people that I consult, a lot of their problems are that like they're just, they never met the true buyer. Right. You know, it's kind of like selling something to a 16 year old kid who really wants, you know, a red Mustang. Of course they want a red Mustang, but if they go in the lot, like they don't have any money, they've got to go convince mom and dad and mom and dad who are the, you know, the true decision makers, true buyer has a totally different view of what they want their son to be driving in. With the, with the waterfall technique, how high do you go? Is there, is it a mistake to bring in the CEO as well? If that's going to, if that's unnecessary? Well, it depends on the size of the organization. Right. So if you take a company like Best Buy, you know, we're looking at say thousands of employees. I like to go to levels above the decision maker. Okay. So, you know, kind of, so three total, the three levels total. You know, if it's a director level, I go, you know, say, you know, VP and then CMO and a really large organization. If it's a much smaller organization and, you know, I want to meet with the CEO. Because I'll often get like, hey, I want to meet with the CEO or the founder. Many times using the same technique, they will, they'll say, hey, I'm the right person to talk to because they don't want you reaching out to the rest of the team. Right. Or, you know, wasting the rest of the team's time. So they'll kind of raise their hand. And then even within that, like the actual message, like every sentence has to be carefully crafted. And what, so the difference between, you know, the salespeople and reviewers that are sending emails using the system and all the other emails is, you know, they spent several hours, you know, learning everything that I've learned, you know, and then applying it to their business and their product and the person that they're, you know, they're writing to, to craft, you know, 12 sentences that, you know, literally every word in that email is, has a purpose. And you have a number of templates and a style, but I guess, you know, can you give us some sense, you know, how, you know, how are these emails different than something we might learn in a copywriting course? Well, the copywriting course isn't going to be focused on email. Like it's not going to be focused on the action of getting somebody to respond in a business environment to schedule a meeting with you. Okay. Right. It's kind of like, what are the words, like if you take copywriting, you know, you could say, okay, well, are we talking about a long form sales letter? Are we talking about a landing page? Are we talking about a, you know, a welcome page, an order page, like whatever, right? There's kind of, are we talking about, you know, a sales letter, like physically sending a letter? You know, every one of those different contexts has kind of a different strategy. There's going to be underlying like psychological persuasion and how do humans think, because how they think is all the same. So, you know, that's going to be similar, but it's not going to be relevant. Like you couldn't just go out and take copywriting courses and say, okay, now I've learned how to email people and get them to respond. You know, there's going to be fundamental similarities as far as like, how do I create an opportunity for the other person? So, they read this and say, man, this looks interesting. I want to talk to, you know, I want to talk to this guy. And that's the decision I want them to make. Or the decision is, this looks interesting. I'm not the guy. This is the guy. You know, you guys go off and handle it. And that's the other thing about organizations. And executives don't do all the work. They've learned to delegate. And, you know, a lot of their job is just delegating to other people. It's not actually doing work. So, you know, let's use that to our advantage once again. It's, there is much a switchboard operator getting everybody communicating from both within the organization and outside the organization as they are a doer of actual work, probably quite a bit more so. Now, so what does, how do we get them to go, wow, this is interesting. I need, I need some, I want to, I want to talk or to have that positive reaction. All right. So, in this email, it's like a three parts, right? So the first part is kind of the intro. I'm writing helps to funding the appropriate person who handles say marketing for the best by example, right? And I know at Best Buy, there's literally 150 people who handle marketing. But I know, but that's actually on purpose. I want it to be vague. And then I'll say, I also wrote to person A, person B, person C in that pursuit. So I tell them that I wrote the other people. I don't CC them. So it makes sense to talk. Let me know your calendar look. And then I go into like my company. All right. So I could say for the example of like BreakthroughEmail.com, BreakthroughEmail.com helps entrepreneurs find leads and increase their revenue. So really quickly, I've said who I am, who I help and how I help them. All right. In one sentence. So that's kind of like the initial structure. My company, who I help, how I help them. And I always tailor it, right? If I'm writing a software service company, BreakthroughEmail helps software service companies find leads and close new business, right? But like it's kind of like a kind of two opportunities. Yeah, I want to find leads. Yeah, I want to close more business. They can't say no to that, right? Especially if I were... And you don't go into an explanation of by sending cold emails and do... You instead, it's the result of what you do is what you're communicating, not getting into the details of how you do it. Well, so that's a good point because that's the next sentence. The challenge with email is somebody's very passively looking at your thing, your email. They're like glancing at the scroll. Yeah, they're like... Your goal is to get them pulled into it. And that's the goal of that paragraph is, hey, gosh, she helps companies like me do these things that I'm interested in, I'll read to the next sentence. And the next sentence is how you do it. Okay. So we do this by through our cold emailing system. And then I go into something called the deep sea fishing technique. Okay. Have you ever been deep sea fishing? Sure. You could easily get sick out there on the way when you lose sight of the horizon. But I have been deep sea fishing. The Mexico was the most recent time. And my mom, my and myself got sick and my dad and my wife just kind of tried to avoid us the whole time. Yeah, I went deep sea fishing in Cozumel and we decided to go out the night before and it was probably like 23 of the time. And taking a bunch of tequila and literally my brother and I were just laying on this boat, just miserable for five hours. So that's what this technique is, is staying up late and drinking a bunch? Yeah, really. So the technique is, so the difference between deep sea fishing and regular fishing, like in a lake or a pond or a river, is if you're in a river, you have one pole in the water. And if you're going deep sea fishing, even though it's laying down in Cozumel on the bench, not paying attention, we still have five or six different lines in the water and we're trolling. So what I'm looking for here is four or five different opportunities for the other person that they might be interested in. And a lot of times people say, hey, here's my one big idea. What do you think? And they do other stuff and they can help people in another way. And so you have to have multiple different opportunities for the other person that you communicate. And you also have to think about, okay, I'm writing say three different levels for people in total. Different people at different levels are thinking about it differently. So I have to speak to these different audiences because I want to create the opportunity to use the Best Buy example for the CMO who's going to think about it much more macro than the director of new media and technology in the Adversary Department. CMO for Best Buy is thinking, okay, we're in Mexico, we're in South America, we're in North America, they handle all that. And so I want to hit multiple people at multiple levels because I know I'm looking for them to delegate it. And for an information marketer, I think most often we get focused on the one thing we want. If we have a JV partner, we want them to promote our launch or we want them to email their list or have us on stage. And I think it's important when you're opening the dialogue, one, if you're doing a launch with a date certain, you've got to do this many months in advance to open a dialogue and build a relationship. But that you provide, like you're saying, four or five opportunities how you can help them and from having maybe promoting them within your own list to providing them a presentation for their in-person event or a guest for a monthly call or a content for their newsletter or have them on a video with you that you promote. So there's, you definitely want, I think it's a brilliant idea to, like you're saying, a deep sea fishing or trolling, having a whole lot of different lines out there within this email to try to capture their interest. Yeah, absolutely. It's actually, it's pretty simple, stupid. Like most of the stuff that we talked about, right? Like, okay, corporations are near lots of levels that people will delegate it down, that people want to cover their butt in the organization. But you really have to think about the other person. Notice, like I haven't been talking about what my product of service was at all. And that was really another point, like working with a lot of people. Like, I don't, we don't really get into their widget or why their widget is so amazing. We get into why their customer thinks that their widget is amazing. Like, what is it that this does? You know, if we think about, like, why people buy, people buy for three reasons. Any idea what the three reasons are? Oh, well, I thought you were going to do the, nobody buys, nobody buys the drill bits, they, for, because they want the drill bits, they buy drill bits because they buy the holes. But, but, right, okay, so. I guess they want to solve a problem. They, or they want to capture an opportunity. And what's the third one? So, the opportunity is right and the problem will further define it. So, the problem is, so the, the opportunity is desire, right? They desire something. Okay. The, the second reason is that they're, they're fearful. So, you know, they'll buy because of fear, which should be a problem. Oh, and the third one, which also to be a problem is that they're frustrated. I typically found that when people bought, you know, like the software, they're advertising or, you know, the whatever, they, they made the decision ultimately because they're frustrated with something. So, you know, they, they wouldn't make, they wouldn't make a decision because, you know, it was interesting, but like it wasn't enough for them to actually make a decision and say yes. And what I'd like to do is I'd like to tether it between desire and frustration. Unless there's an intrinsic fear, like fear losing out. But for another part in an, in an email, there isn't, you're not going to have some sort of fear over the other person. At least in this context, for like joint ventures and partnerships and things like that. So it was like, what's the opportunity for them? Well, the opportunity could be, as you said, like to be on your list and be promoted. Like just think about like what it is you want, if your profile is kind of similar to them. Like, well, I want somebody to promote me. I want somebody to bring me new customers. I want to do speaking events. I want to people to promote my products. Just say that to the other person. That you can help them promote their products that you can, you know, as you said, you can invite them to, you know, speaking events. You can't like whatever it is. And all of a sudden your email, which normally is like, hey, can you give me this? Can you give me this? Can you give me this? I want this. It is now an opportunity for the other person. And it's, you know, it's a much better way to at least start the conversation. Because if it's some sort of partnership, it's like, okay, well, what do you do? What do I do? Let's see if it makes sense for us to work together or not. And with information market or some of the other reasons, you know, they, you know, they need content for their, you know, calls and, and, and newsletters. And so by offering to help them, you know, that's, that's kind of a frustration is, you know, they got, they got to fill this newsletter. And if you're offering to provide content, then that could be a, you know, that could be beneficial to them. Also, when approaching JVs, a lot of folks just kind of start with the money and say, hey, I got an opportunity for you to make a lot of money with this launch or with this product. And, and certainly money is important and it is a factor that they will consider. But they're also very protective. If they've been in business at all for any length of time, they're going to be very protective of their members. And what I found is, you know, they're more open when you talk about how your program or content is going to help their customers and, and that also make them money. Yeah, absolutely. I mean, you're very protective of your list. Right. You know, right now I have 11,000 subscribers and, you know, if somebody's like, comes in on like, you know, cost per lead or cost per click, you know, it's like, okay, interesting, but like, what is this product? You know, I don't want to sell snake oil because, you know, in a couple emails, I can lose a lot of credibility with this audience. And, you know, I want to partner with people who have things that can help the group that, you know, that I, that I work with and can make their life work better. And hey, if I can partner with that company and maybe make some money, that's great too. But I'm not going to sacrifice, you know, this, this asset and this relationship that I built with this group over, you know, some sort of cost per lead. And I found that that's actually very little what these joint membership partners really care about, like at least the top guys. You know, surprisingly, I was told that that's what they cared about. And, you know, in reality, you know, when I went to, when I went to them, it really wasn't, you know, they wanted to learn something that they didn't know and they wanted to really feel like what they were bringing had just a ton of value to their audience. That they were excited about themselves. Yeah. And, and could really get behind them back. Yeah, I think that's, that's the key that you mentioned is that, you know, that and now within your email system, how would you integrate that within the deep sea fishing? You know, now that we kind of talked around the deep sea fishing, how would you kind of add a list of things that people would, would, that we respond to for one of these, these big partners? How would I add a list? What would you kind of say in that email to, to get a JV sponsor interested? What would be the four or five things that you would talk about? Well, it totally depends on who it is I'm writing. Right. Can you come up with an example of somebody to contact? Well, he's, you know, Frank Kern is doing a huge launch right now. So, you know, he's going to have a big list and, you know, so what, what if we were approaching him to, to promote our, our emailing system? So the first thing I would do is I would look for a referral from somebody. Do you know Frank Kern? Sure. Do you think it would make sense for us to talk about what I do? Of course. Yeah. Okay. Good. Do you mind sending it? Would you mind sending an email to Frank to introduce us? I can. He doesn't necessarily respond to his email. Okay. So like, but I'd be, but we can't, he may not, he may not respond to your emails. Like it's a good relationship, but not like, you know, tight. Yeah. Yeah. I mean, it just, it depends. You know, sometimes, you know, just depend, you know, I can't, I don't know, but I'd be happy to send an email for you. Sure. Would it make sense for me to kind of send you a quick kind of overview of what we do and you can kind of forward it to make it easier for you? I think, yeah, I think that would probably be the best. Okay. So I'm going to stop. So that is what I would do. Okay. The first thing I would do, like, so I, I went with a, my first launch was within, you know, a well-known marketer within the space, probably like two years ago. And I think it might be the first you ever heard of me until we met in person recently, but I got an opinion referral from somebody and I used that same technique, which was, you know, hey, do you mind giving me an introduction? Would it make sense for me to, you know, to talk to that person? And, you know, I turned a 10-minute meeting into a 40-minute meeting, which we now, we've been spent a half a day together in person. And the reason that he wanted to do this is because he had created a whole system because he hated copyrighting systems. He hated a cold call. So he was like interested in it and he thought it would be a lot of value. And this was before I even had a product or, you know, a training. So the first thing I always do is I look for referrals. And the great thing about the informational marketing space is it's like high school. Everybody knows everybody. And, and you're solving a problem that they have that very often that the, the joint venture partner you're trying to reach actually has. Well, you know, they don't know if I'm solving a problem yet. I think I'm vetted. And that's kind of the thing that everyone's trying to decide. Like who's in the, and that's why I call it like high school because like who's in the cool crowd, like who's, you know, well respected. And then who are the second level and the third level, right? So if you get to kind of those top levels, I'm always asking for a referral. And I ask for a referral the same way every time. You know, like, hey, based on what you know about us, can you think of anyone else that might make sense for me to talk to? So whether I'm in a large organization or, you know, and, and just kind of it's very soft. It's like, you know, what, like, can you think of anybody? And, and they'll just fill down their list of, you know, 500 contacts to say here are the water to people that for whatever reason, I think you guys would be a great fit together. That's cool. Now, in answer to your earlier question, like what do you actually say to Frank Kern? You know, I think Frank Kern's audience and my audience are a little bit different. But I would talk about like credibility of like other people that I've worked with in the past. You know, I would, the first thing I would probably do is I'd invite Frank to, you know, come to my list and, you know, reach out to them and talk to them about what he's doing, especially if he's got a promotion going on and develop some sort of relationship with him, where at some point he's like, OK, this is a good guy. Like, how else could we work together? OK. Would be my strategy. So, so it's the goal is to build a relationship with that first email, not as much. You got to kind of get to first base before you can get to second and certainly before you can get all the way home and work to start a relationship rather than coming right out and saying, hey, I want, I want you to promote me. Absolutely. I mean, you, I think the baseball analysis is really good. I mean, getting the first base is like getting them to respond or getting a meeting with them. Right. So, where they're at right now is they don't know you. They don't know who you are. They don't know if it's worth their time. Is it worth their time, which is really what, what you're trying to overcome at this point? Not do they want to make a decision on this, which is say, you know, third base or home run. And you just need to get like the, I think it was you, like the nose of the camel under the tent. Yeah. Yeah. I mean, that's like, I love that. But there's a lot more to this. There's like the scheduling of it. There's what you do in the meeting, which we have a whole process around that. And then there's also follow up, you know, people follow up is a big problem where they have all these people that have these good conversations and they're like, how do I follow up? What do I say?
